entry.title: How to Configure Remote ATC Between SAP BTP ABAP Environment and On‑Premise S/4HANA (Step‑by‑Step)?
entry.content:
* Main issue is that I am not able to  find " Object provider for remote system" under custom code analysis tile.

Cloud Connector RFC Mapping – Not Sure Which Settings to Use

When adding an on‑premise ABAP system in Cloud Connector (Protocol = RFC), I am asked to choose:

  • With load balancing (SID + message server)
  • Without load balancing (app server + instance number)

I am unsure which one is correct for Remote ATC, and what the exact host/port values should be.

Communication System in BTP ABAP Does Not Show RFC Option

After setting up the Cloud Connector entries, when I create a Communication System in the BTP ABAP Environment, the RFC outbound service is missing, and I cannot create the RFC destination required for Remote ATC.

Object Providers Do Not Appear in Custom Code Migration App

In the Custom Code Analysis Project setup screen,
“Object Provider to Remote System” shows No data available, meaning I cannot continue the Remote ATC configuration.

What Is the Correct End‑to‑End Setup?

I am following SAP’s Remote ATC documentation (Cloud Connector + Communication System + Object Provider), but there seems to be missing prerequisites or configuration steps.

Can someone guide me with the correct end‑to‑end steps?

Specifically:

  • Which RFC configuration should be used in Cloud Connector (load balancing or not)?
  • What are the correct message server / app server details for S/4HANA?
  • How to ensure the Communication System in BTP ABAP recognizes the RFC connection?
  • How to make Object Providers appear under ATC Administration → Setup?

Any screenshots, step-by-step instructions, or clarifications from someone who has successfully configured Remote ATC would be greatly appreciated.

I tried to follow few SAP blogs but they are very old to follow up.

Thank you!

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

I must admit that I’m feeling quite stuck custom code analysis catalog inside Analyze custom code analysis tile , as I can’t find any proper end‑to‑end documentation. The SAP Help Portal only provides fragmented information, and none of the pages explain the full process in a clear, sequential way. Could you please share any link or document where all the steps are described in order? Main blocker is when I go inside the catalog Custom Code Analysis -> Custom code analysis tile -> Create custom code analysis project "Object provider to remote system" is blank.How to get it.

entry.title: Dark Mode Beta Phase Complete: See What's New
entry.content:
We are excited to announce that Dark Mode is now live as the Beta Phase is complete! 

Last year, after listening to your feedback, we launched Dark Mode in beta, giving you the chance to choose your preferred visual experience. 

Since then, you have shared suggestions on how to make this feature more polished and seamless. With that in mind, we are happy to introduce the following improvements: 

  1. Light/Dark Mode Toggle 

A new toggle replaces the temporary text-based switch, making it easier to switch between themes for a more personalized browsing experience.

 

  1. Dark Mode Improvements for DSA Reporting Form 

The "Report Inappropriate Content" form has been updated to ensure all table content is visible and readable in dark mode. Previously unreadable white-on-white elements now follow the correct dark theme styling. 

Why this matters?  

Dark Mode improves how you experience the SAP Community everyday:  

  • More comfortable browsing: Reduced eye strain, especially in low-light environments, makes it easier to stay engaged for longer.   
  • Greater control over your experience: The new toggle lets you instantly switch between light and dark modes, so you can choose what works best for you at any moment.   
  • Better readability where it counts: Improvements to key areas like the reporting form to ensure content is always clear and accessible, no matter the theme.

entry.title: SAP Convergent Mediation by DigitalRoute – New Release Version 9.5
entry.content:
SAP Convergent Mediation by DigitalRoute – New Release Version 9.5

New Release

Public

March 2026

We are excited to announce the release and general availability of SAP Convergent Mediation 9.5!

With this release, we continue our focus on three key value areas: productivity and efficiency, quality and reliability, and security and compliance. It introduces enhancements that simplify configuration management and deployment across environments, extend persistence options for real-time workflows, and strengthen control of workflow execution.

These capabilities enable more consistent automation in CI/CD pipelines, ensure predictable and transparent operations, and support secure, compliant handling of data and configurations across all environments. 

Some of the key features and enhancements are:

SQL for Aggregation Storage in Real-time Workflows

This enhancement adds SQL as a storage option for aggregation in real-time workflows. Previously, SQL storage only existed for batch workflows.

With SQL storage, you can persist session data in real-time workflows without relying on disk, Couchbase or Redis. The feature reuses and extends functionality already developed for batch aggregation, adapting it for real-time workflow scenarios.

For SAP ECS customers this adds support for managing and storing real-time workflow run-time aggregation data on HANA database instead of filesystem. Thus allowing disaster recovery replication process to be more frequent as it's based on HANA database instead of filesystem replication.

Direct Creation of Database Tables

This enhancement enables you to create the required database tables for Aggregation, Duplicate UDR, Inter Workflow, and Data Veracity directly from the Desktop, and not have to ask your DBA to create the tables for you. Further lowering the threshold for customers that want to store all their run-time data on HANA database instead of filesystem, for more frequent replication of run-time data to disaster recovery site.

Previously, creating or modifying tables in some environments required the generated SQL code to be run separately. This could mean submitting it through service tickets for execution, causing delays and extending project lead times. With this feature, you can initiate and complete table creation requests directly in the profile configuration, reducing dependency on external tools and support processes. This supports productivity, faster project delivery, and improved usability.

REST Interface for Execution Contexts

This enhancement lets you monitor and deactivate standalone workflows even when the platform server is offline—crucial during upgrades when workflows continue running independently. The extended Operations REST interface provides methods to query workflow details and deactivate them, supporting automation and integration with operational tools. Existing properties still control interface availability.

By removing platform dependency for workflow management and enabling direct, automated control, this feature ensures reliable operations during upgrades or planned downtime.

SOC 1/SOX Guidance

If you are subject to SOC 1/SOX compliance, you may have been asked: 

How does SAP Convergent Mediation help me demonstrate SOC 1 control operation? 

In this release, we are publishing a set of pages that map MediationZone capabilities to SOC 1/SOX control objectives, so you can show how the product supports your financial reporting controls. 

For the System Properties, Desktop Properties, and Legacy Desktop properties, we indicate: 

  1. Whether it is in scope for SOC 1.
  2. The primary categories supported are Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability.
  3. The Reason, a short explanation of the control value, for example, “enforces encryption,” “prevents resource exhaustion,” or “standardizes log timestamps for audit”. This helps you baseline configurations.

Around the mapping, we have added information to help you put these controls in operation:

  • Change Management explains how to route MediationZone configuration changes through approvals and capture evidence
  • Sensitive Access Monitoring outlines approaches for governing administrator access, permissions, and events
  • User Access Monitoring covers provisioning, lifecycle, and the kinds of artifacts auditors typically request.

Each section clarifies the split of responsibilities: what SAP Convergent Mediation provides, what you configure, and what must be proven by your own processes. If you are preparing for a SOX cycle, start at the SOC 1 and SOX Guidance page. Use the properties mapping to set or verify your configuration baseline, then pull the Change/Sensitive Access/User Access pages into your control narratives and evidence plans.

We hope this guidance saves your team time and gives your auditors the confidence they need. 

We encourage you to explore all the detailed features and enhancements below to gain a comprehensive understanding of the capabilities in MediationZone 9.5:

  • SOC 1 / SOX Compliance Guidance

New Features

Usability

  • Direct Creation of Database Tables for Data Veracity, Aggregation, Inter Workflow and Duplicate UDR
  • Topic Creation for Kafka Batch Transaction Handler

Additional Functionality Made Available in Desktop

  • Adding Comments to Workflows and Agents
  • ECS Inspector Bulk Edit and Restricted Fields

Integration and Compliance

  • SQL Aggregation Storage for Real-time Workflows
  • SOC 1 / SOX Compliance Guidance

Functionality Improvements

  • REST Interface in EC
  • SOAP 1.2 Support for Web Service
  • External Reference Support for Diameter Routing Profile
  • Load Balancer Added to the TCP/IP Processing Agent

Usability Improvements

  • Inline Editing Added to Workflow Table

Documentation Improvements

  • New Kafka documentation improvements
  • Clarification of Terminology for WorkflowKey 

More information 

  • SAP Product Page and Product Video 
  • SAP Convergent Mediation 9.5 Release (DigitalRoute InfoZone)
  • SAP Convergent Mediation 9.5 Release Notes (DigitalRoute InfoZone)
  • SAP Convergent Mediation 9.5 Documentation (DigitalRoute InfoZone)
  • SAP Convergent Mediation 9.5 Composite SAP Note and SAP CM Help Portal page
  • SAP Convergent Mediation 4.x End of Mainstream Maintenance (describing the need for customer still on SAP Convergent Mediation 4.x to upgrade to latest SAP Convergent Mediation release)

Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Missing Attachments in Singapore GST...
entry.content:
Dear all,

Please note that the India GSTR reports have different functionality compared to the Singapore GST Return report, as both are designed in accordance with country-specific regulatory requirements.

Based on our internal verification, Singapore GST reports are generated without the attachment of tax line items, which is in line with the standard system behavior.

Additionally, the structure and level of detail in these reports may vary due to differences in statutory reporting formats, data aggregation logic, and compliance guidelines defined for each country.

 

 

Best regards,

Atanu

SAP Support - S/4HANA Cloud

entry.title: SAP MDG inbound BP webservice, does not copy LongPartTaxId value from payload to staging area
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We are facing an issue in processing the inbound BP webservice in SAP MDG.

After receiving BP supplier webservice from a third party system into MDG, the LongPartyTaxId value is not getting copied into staging area of the CR.

After debugging the implementaion class of Badi MDG_SE_BP_BULK_REPLRQ_IN, i found that the value from payload is not copied into LongPartyTaxId field in the IN parameter and therefore not passed into staging area and it leads to error 'Enter tax number for tax category'.

This issue happens only when payload contains tax type category 5 and above.

Kindly let me know the potential reason behind this.

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Configuring and testing Production Planning (PP) APIs from Accelerator Hub in SAP On-Premise
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am looking for some guidance on how to properly configure and test OData APIs found on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub within my SAP On-Premise system.

Context: I am working with the Production Planning (PP) module and need to extract master data and configuration details. Specifically, I am looking to use APIs to get data related to:

  • Plants and associated products/materials

  • Work Centers

  • Routings and Production Versions

  • ......

My goal is to expose this PP data so it can be consumed by an external application.

My Environment:

  • System: SAP On-Premise SAP S/4HANA 

My Questions:

  1. API Identification: Which specific standard OData services/APIs on the Accelerator Hub are recommended for extracting this combination of PP master data (Work Centers, Routings, Plant/Product configurations)?

  2. On-Premise Configuration: What are the exact steps to activate these APIs in my on-premise system? (e.g., finding the correct backend service and activating it via transaction /IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE).

  3. Roles & Authorizations: Are there specific standard PFCG roles or authorization objects required for a system user to read this PP data via the API?

  4. Testing: Once the OData service is active, what is the best way to test the endpoints and payloads? (e.g., using SAP Gateway Client /IWFND/GW_CLIENT or Postman).

If anyone has experience setting up these specific Production Planning APIs on-premise, or can share a step-by-step blog or SAP Note, I would highly appreciate the help!

Thank you in advance,

entry.title: SAP SYSTEM ARCHIVAL CONFIGURATION - SARA - PART 2 #ATR
entry.content:
#ATR (25)

Informative Note:

  • Created this blog to provide information on SAP System Archival Operation done on DBTABLOG Technical Table (BC_DBLOGS - Archiving Object).
  • Archive DBTABLOG Table Data till May 2024 (Technically 1 Month Data)

CHECK POINTS:
PART 1:

  • Table Information
  • Archival Prerequisite Checks
  • Archiving Configuration

Refer link - SAP SYSTEM ARCHIVAL CONFIGURATION - SARA - PART 1 #ATR

PART 2:

  • Archival Trail Run Methods (Info)
  • Archival Run
  • Post Archival Checks
  • Archival – Read Retrieval Check

ARCHIVAL TRAIL RUN METHODS [ADDITIONAL INFO – TAKEN ON ANOTHER SCENARIO]

  • In my case, we are going to set direct Archival Run and Deletion to have Archival Config reside permanently.
  • For Safer Runs, Test Runs can be executed as well before setting direct deletion (taken on another scenario – out of our case)

Archival Test Run:

  • If we run Test Mode, Archive files/deletion won’t occur.
  • We can determine data which will archive, and sufficient space required for the Archival Run.

Archival Run without deletion:

  • If we run Production Mode with “Delete with Test Variant”, then Data will be archived but deletion won’t occur since we used “Delete Program: Test Mode Variant - SAP&_TEST_DEL – which won’t delete data”

  • We can run deletion job alone to complete archiving session once we verified/confirmed archival jobs (data archived) completed fine. Data will be deleted from table.

CHOOSE YOUR PROCEDURE ACCORDINGLY BASED ON THE CRITICALITY OF THE DATA

METHOD 1: With Archival Test Run (1) - > Archival Run without deletion (2) -> Deletion Job Alone (3)

  • Set Write Job as Test Mode. Delete Program Variant – Optional since it’s test mode run.
  • Set Write Job as Production Mode + Delete with Test Variant, Delete Program: Test Mode Variant as Test Run (SAP&_TEST_DEL).
  • Run Deletion Job Alone – DELETE from SARA – BC_DBLOGS.

METHOD 2: With Direct Archival Run without Deletion (1) -> Deletion Job Alone (2)

  • Set Write Job as Production Mode + Delete with Test Variant, Delete Program: Test Mode Variant as Test Run (SAP&_TEST_DEL).
  • Run Deletion Job Alone – DELETE from SARA – BC_DBLOGS.

METHOD 3: With Direct Archival and deletion (1)   --- OUR SCENARIO – Permanent Configuration

  • Set Write Job as Production Mode, Delete Program: Live Mode Variant as Production Run (SAP&_PROD_DEL)
  • Archival operation takes place via Write Job post which Data will be deleted from table via DELETE job.

CHECK POINT 4: ARCHIVAL RUN

4.1 - 2018 Year Data Archival:
(No Data)

  • Go to “SARA” transaction. Provide BC_DBLOGS object and select “WRITE” job.

  • Provide Z_ARCH_2018 variant.
  • Choose Start Date – Immediate Job.

  • Choose Spool Parameters – Print Time – SAP Spool Only for now.

  • Execute to trigger archival (write job)

  • Check SM37 Job.

  • Since no data exists, Archiving delete job didn’t trigger.

4.2 - 2019 – 2023 Year Data Archival

  • Repeat the same process for rest of the Variants – 2019 – 2023 (NIL Data)

Z_ARCH_2019 – Z_ARCH_2023:

4.3 - JAN – MAY 2024 Data Archival

  • Now, we can proceed to archive real data - ZARV_JAN_MAY24
  • PERIOD - JAN 2024 – MAY 2024 in which data resides from 28.04.2024 onwards.
  • Approx Entries which will be archived - 27.03.2018 – 31.05.2024 - 19.040.314 Entries

  • We could see Archival Write jobs completed successfully.
  • To delete data from table - Deletion Job triggered once each archival files (with 500MB) created.
  • We could see 19.040.314 Entries Archived and Deleted from table as expected.

Check Point 4 ARCHIVAL RUN Completed.

CHECK POINT 5: POST ARCHIVAL CHECKS

5.1 - DBTABLOG Table Size:

  • DB02 – DBTABLOG Size – 234.30 G – 53.252.506 Entries.  (Check Date - 18.09.2024)

5.2 - FS Utilization:

  • Current FS Utilization in /usr/sap/archives – 5.9G

  • Go to “SARA” transaction – Provide BC_DBLOGS Archiving Object – Select Statistics Tab.

5.3 - Archiving Session Management Check

  • Go to “SARA” transaction – Provide BC_DBLOGS Archiving Object – Select Management Tab.

5.4 - Logs Check:

  • Go to “SARA” transaction – Provide BC_DBLOGS Archiving Object – Select Logs Tab.

5.5 - Job Spool log checks:

  • Check Write and Deletion Job “Spool Logs” for detailed archiving session information.

Write Job log reference:

Deletion Job log reference:

Check Point 5 POST ARCHIVAL CHECKS Completed.

CHECK POINT 6: ARCHIVE READ – RETRIEVAL CHECK

  • Go to “SARI” transaction – Select “Status”.
  • Check Status Per Archive as well as Status per Infostructure.

  • Go to “Archive Explorer” – Provide our BC_DBLOGS Archive Infostructure – SAP_BC_DBLOGS.
  • We could see correct Entries Count calculated/picked from the archived files directory (/usr/sap/archives).

  • We can filter dates accordingly and read archived data.

Check Point 6 ARCHIVE READ – RETRIEVAL CHECK Completed

*** WE HAVE SUCCESSFULLY PERFORMED SAP SYSTEM ARCHIVAL OPERATION***

Thanks for Visiting !!!

entry.title: Innovation in Action: The Exhibition at the SAP for Energy and Utilities in Toulouse
entry.content:
This year, the Exhibition once again stands as one of the central highlights of the SAP Conference for Energy and Utilities., taking
 place between April 21-23, 2026 in Toulouse. In addition to numerous expert sessions and discussions, the exhibition area offers a rich experience shaped by innovative technologies, a strong partner ecosystem, and practical use cases from across the energy sector.

A Diverse Exhibition: SAP and Strong Partners

Visitors can look forward to a broad ecosystem of solutions: alongside SAP, many partners will showcase technologies tailored to the specific needs of the energy and utilities industry. Together, we demonstrate how the sector is transforming and how modern digital solutions enable new business models while optimizing existing processes.

Get a preview of the partners taking part in the conference, along with solution demos showcasing some of the most relevant SAP and partner solutions for the Utilities and Oil, Gas & Energy industries:

 

 

 

 
 
 
 
 
 
A special highlight is the Distributed Energy Resources (DER) Booth, a shared space featuring multiple partners.
Here, visitors can explore how SAP DER powers the energy‑flexibility market with a trusted data backbone that unifies assets, agreements, and energy data. By enabling the orchestration of millions of distributed devices, SAP helps utilities deliver coordinated energy services such as energy communities and virtual power plants. 
Together with Enode, Exnaton, Volue, and Evolution Energie, the booth presents a comprehensive ecosystem covering residential flexibility, energy sharing, grid capacity and congestion management, as well as portfolio management and energy trading. 

 

The Exhibition: Explore, Experience, Understand

 

The Exhibition invites attendees not just to observe technology, but to experience it firsthand. Through the conference app, visitors can select from more than 50 solution demos across Utilities and Oil, Gas & Energy. Each demo is presented by an SAP expert who is available to answer questions directly and provide tailored insights.

An extended selection of demos featured in the Exhibition:

SAP Solutions for Utilities

  • Utilities Customer Self‑Service Agent
  • SAP Business Data Cloud
  • SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 – Utilities Add‑On
  • SAP FIORI Apps: Billing and Meter Reading
  • Smart Water Platform – Remote Water Quality Monitoring & Leakage Detection
  • Leverage SAP’s Toolchain and Utilities Content to Accelerate Your Transformation
  • SAP Customer Engagement (formerly SAP Emarsys)

Solutions for Oil, Gas & Energy

  • Digital Twins Driving the Future of Business
  • Business AI Use Cases and Accelerators for Oil, Gas & Energy
  • SAP Field Logistics – Efficiently Manage Upstream Logistics
  • Secondary Distribution for Oil, Gas, and Energy

Solutions for both industries

  • Autonomous Maintenance / AI‑Assisted Maintenance Work Orders & Notifications
  • Business AI for Energy and Utilities – AI for Asset Management

These demos cover a wide spectrum, from customer experience to asset management, connected operations, AI‑enabled maintenance, and end‑to‑end process optimization across the energy landscape.

A further Highlight: Our “Next Era of Energy and Utilities” Showcase

Our Next Era of Energy & Utilities showcase shows how energy companies can shift from reactive operations to proactive, insight‑driven management. With SAP Business Suite, SAP Business Data Cloud, agentic AI, CX solutions, and intelligent asset management, executives gain real‑time visibility across the enterprise. CROs can drive personalized customer experiences and loyalty, while CFOs connect financial and sustainability data to protect margins and steer profitability. Customers can choose between the Utilities or Oil, Gas & Energy Enterprise storyline and experience how SAP enables a sustainable, resilient, and future‑ready energy enterprise. Visit us at the booth, no booking required to experience this showcase.

 

We Look Forward to Connecting With You

The entire SAP Exhibition Team is excited to engage with visitors throughout the event, ready to answer your questions, explore ideas, and discuss new perspectives for the future of the energy and utilities industry. 

Looking forward to seeing you in Toulouse! 🇫🇷

entry.title: Re: Verification of Payee (VoP) - What is planned in SAP S/4HANA Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Hi @AndreaRi 

Thanks a lot for the suggestion, it sounds like a very good approach.

However, I have a question regarding how this would work in practice. When I run payments through Manage Automatic Payments, I don’t see an option to generate something like a payment proposal or XML without posting.

In order to generate the payment file (XML), I need to click on “Schedule Payment”, and at that point the postings are created automatically.

Could you please clarify how the process with payment orders would work in this case? Is it handled through a different app or configuration?

Also, do you have any documentation or examples that could help us better understand how to implement this?

Thanks a lot for your help!

Best regards,
Sandra

entry.title: Can LLMs find unusual patterns in a text
entry.content:
On this April’s Fools’ Day 2026, I decided to have a frivolous conversation with an Ai agent. Let’s see how it went…

 

Discussion with GPT-5

My user prompt

Explain the uncommon rule that was used to write the 3 following phrases.

Phrase 1: Kids like marshmallows.

Phrase 2: After becoming clerk, Douglas E Ferguson got humbly in joy.

Phrase 3: Her inmate Janis knows literature, macrame, not opera.

 

The answer from the machine

Each phrase is an abecedarian: the initial letters of consecutive words follow the alphabet in order.

Phrase 2: After (A) becoming (B) clerk (C), Douglas (D) E (E) Ferguson (F) got (G) humbly (H) in (I) joy (J).

Phrase 3: Her (H) inmate (I) Janis (J) knows (K) literature (L), macrame (M), not (N) opera (O).

Phrase 1: Kids (K) like (L) marshmallows (M).

So the “uncommon rule” is that each successive word starts with the next letter of the alphabet.

I could have replied to the machine: GPT, that’s super relevant, thanks! But I kept my tokens for the next discussion.

 

Discussion with Gemini 2.5 Pro

My user prompt

The 3 dialogue excerpts below have the same unusual constraint; generate 3 new dialogue excerpts that all respect that constraint.

Dialogue excerpt 1: Pick Kevin next time!

Dialogue excerpt 2: Never regret, Tina, and don’t theorize endlessly.

Dialogue excerpt 3: Yes. Suddenly you understand Diego’s situation.

 

The answer from the machine

The unusual constraint is that the last letter of each word is the same as the first letter of the following word (ignoring case and punctuation).

Here are 3 new dialogue excerpts that respect that constraint:

Dialogue excerpt 1: Stop playing games, Sam.

Dialogue excerpt 2: We expect trouble elsewhere.

Dialogue excerpt 3: Did David do our report, Thomas?

Such rule is easy to find for the human eye, likewise for today’s Ai agents. Note that my dialogues were hand-made. But what about the ones from the Ai agent? If I am not mistaken, Gemini’s dialogues are not just a copy from the internet (I did a web search to check), they are hand-made, assuming Ai agents have hands.

 

Discussion with Claude 4.5 Sonnet and others

My User Prompt

The 4 following sentences have the same uncommon pattern; produce 3 new sentences that all follow that pattern.

Sentence 1: Ohio area, 2002, Dad sees Mom.

Sentence 2: Samuel’s kayak did engage yesterday.

Sentence 3: Noon, Roger says miam-miam.

Sentence 4: Yearly species died.

 

Answer from Claude

Looking at these sentences, I can see they are all palindromes - they read the same forwards and backwards letter by letter (ignoring spaces, punctuation, and capitalization).

Let me verify:

"Ohio area, 2002, Dad sees Mom" → ohioarea2002dadseesmom

"Samuel's kayak did engage yesterday" → samuelskayakdidengageyesterday

"Noon, Roger says miam-miam" → noonrogersaysmiammiam

"Yearly species died" → yearlyspeciesdied

Here are 3 new sentences following this pattern:

  1. Was it a rat I saw?
  2. Madam, I'm Adam.
  3. A man, a plan, a canal: Panama.

Each word in my sentences starts and ends with the same letter. Claude and other reasoning LLMs failed to find such pattern, at least in a one-shot prompt. Having locally in a sentence some palindromes like: 2002, Dad, sees, Mom, kayak, did, noon, does not mean the global sentence is itself a palindrome. It appears that the 3 sentences written by the Ai agent here, are not hand-made but a copy from the internet.

 

 Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

The uncommon pattern is that if you read the entire sentence backward (character by character, including spaces), it forms a new, grammatically coherent sentence made up of the reverse of the original words. This is a type of sentence-level semordnilap.

For example, "Yearly species died." read backward is ".deid seiceps ylraeY", which is the sentence "Died species Yearly."

Here are 3 new sentences that follow that same pattern:

  1. Emit no evil. (Reverses to: "live on timE.")
  2. Draw a reward. (Reverses to: "drawer a warD.")
  3. Stop! Murder us not. (Reverses to: ".ton su redruM !potS")

The word semordnilap found in the answer is not an invention from the Ai agent: see https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/semordnilap

 

The non-answer from GPT5

The machine took time to “reason” but gave no answer back: I might have reached my quota.

 

 

Happy April Fools' Day

entry.title: Re: How to avoid latency in the Compensation Module
entry.content:
Hi @andoniainaMestrallet it's not about the numbers of employees overall, it's about the number of employees on any worksheet. One worksheet with 150 employees will likely create latency in executive review. Can you confirm there isn't more than 20-30 employees per worksheet ?

Can you also confirm the template isn't tied to a lookup table with thousands of values in it ?

Finally are you using the feature "Executive Review based on Route map" from 2H 2024 and if so, do you also have very large numbers of inactive employees when you export the UDF ?

 

If you think everything above is ok, I think you should log a ticket and provide the x-unique-id as mentioned at the bottom.

Thanks

entry.title: SAP DSP: persist API TASK payload in a local table
entry.content:
Dear SAP colleagues,

I'm experimenting the API task option in SAP DSP Task Chain.

I've configured correctly my HTTP with the proper authentication and I'm able to retrieve data from a specific internal and private API.

However, my objective would be persist those data in a physical table so that I can integrate those data into my data domain.

I've tried to create an SQL Script Procedure on the Open SQL Schema, with Input Parameter a string (that should be my payload) that is inserting the data into a DB table.

 I'm not able to pass the API Response of the first task to the SQL Procedure as input parameter.

Do you know if there's any possibility to do that or I'm doing something totally insane?

Thanks and regards,

i

entry.title: Dynamically filter your SAC Story widgets for a BW Query
entry.content:
As my BW Live model in the practice system (if you want to do this on your own jump to the last section of this artice) has data only between the years 2015 and 2017, I need to use an offset between 9 and 11 years, so I created a dynamic time filtering in SAP Analytics Cloud Optimized Story to use auto-current month (YYYYMM) with a 9–11 year slider. This is what would be needed if you go against a BW datasource.

What you’ll build

  • Compute the current month in YYYYMM in script.
  • Apply that month as a filter to both a table and a chart via the Optimized Story scripting API.
  • Add a slider that lets users jump back 9, 10, or 11 years from “now.”
  • Keep table and chart synchronized by applying the same time filter to both data sources.

Prerequisites

  • You are using the Optimized Story Experience with scripting enabled.
  • Your model’s time dimension has member IDs in YYYYMM format (for example, 202403). If not, adjust the formatter/mapping.
  • You’ve enabled technical names for widgets and dimensions so you can reference them in scripts.
  • You have a table (for example, Table_1) and a chart (Chart_1) bound to the same model.
  • You can add a scripting Slider control (Slider_YearsBack) and optional text label (Text_CurrentSelection).

Key Optimized Story scripting APIs used

  • Widget.getDataSource()
  • DataSource.setDimensionFilter(dimensionId, membersArray)
  • Slider.setMinValue(number), setMaxValue(number), setStep(number), setValue(number), getValue()
  • Event handlers: Page.onInitialization, Slider.onChange
  • Text.setText(string) [optional]

Step-by-step

  1. Add the widgets
  • Insert a chart (Chart_1) and a table (Table_1) bound to your model.
  • Create a Script Variable SV_YearsBack
  • Insert a Slider control (Slider_YearsBack).
  • Optionally insert a Text widget (Text_1) to show the current filter.
  • Confirm the technical name of your time dimension (example: Date). You’ll use it in code.
  1. Create a Script Object helper
    Create a Script function filterWidgets and paste this script. It uses standard JavaScript plus Optimized Story scripting API calls.
// Current Date (Today)

var today = new Date();

console.log(today);



// Format dates manually for display and console

var todayMonth = today.getMonth() + 1;

var todayDay = today.getDate();

var todayYear = today.getFullYear();

var filteredYear = todayYear- SV_YearsBack;

var todayStr = todayMonth.toString() + "/" + todayDay.toString() + "/" + todayYear.toString();

console.log(todayStr);



// For current month

var todayMonthPadded = "0" + todayMonth.toString();

todayMonthPadded = todayMonthPadded.slice(-2);

var filteredYYYYMM = filteredYear.toString() + todayMonthPadded;

console.log("Filter to YYYYMM: " +filteredYYYYMM);

Chart_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("4SACDEMO_CP-YEAR_",filteredYYYYMM);

Table_1.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("4SACDEMO_CP-YEAR_",filteredYYYYMM);

Text_1.applyText("go back "+ ConvertUtils.numberToString(SV_YearsBack)+ " years");

 

  1. Initialize on story (or page) load
    Use the Page_1 onInitialization event to apply the initial filter, via calling the function
  1. React to slider changes
    Open the Slider_YearsBack’s onChange event and call the script again.

 

That’s it. Each time the story loads, you compute the current month and default to 9 years back. Users can switch to 10 or 11 years back, and the table and chart update in sync using DataSource.setDimensionFilter.

Notes and variations

  • If your time IDs aren’t YYYYMM: Map the computed date to your model’s IDs before calling setDimensionFilter (for example, convert to 2024.P03 for fiscal period 3).
  • Use months instead of years: Configure the slider between 108 and 132, set step to 1, and pass Slider_YearsBack.getValue() directly to offsetYYYYMM without multiplying by 12.
  • Filter a range (rolling window): Build an array of YYYYMM values from the target month up to current, then pass that array to setDimensionFilter. Be mindful of performance for long ranges.
  • Story-wide behavior: If you prefer one control to drive multiple widgets via linked analysis, set the filter on a single widget that is the driver. In Optimized Story, scripting a story-level filter is limited compared to Analytic Applications, so setting the filter on each widget’s DataSource is a reliable approach.
  • Missing members: If the computed member doesn’t exist (for example, gaps in historical data), decide whether to look up the nearest available month or clear the filter. You can handle this with a pre-check using available member lists or by showing a friendly message.

Testing checklist

  • Verify that the story defaults to “current month minus 9 years” after load.
  • Move the slider to 10 and 11; ensure both chart and table refresh.
  • Check boundary behavior in January (correctly falls back into last year).
  • Validate results against known historical periods.

Troubleshooting

  • No data after filter: Ensure the computed YYYYMM exists as a member ID. Adjust the formatter or map to the model’s ID scheme.
  • Filter not applied: Confirm both widgets are bound to the same model and that you’re calling getDataSource().setDimensionFilter on each widget.
  • Timezone differences: The “current month” uses the runtime environment’s clock. If you must pin to a specific timezone or fiscal period, expose a parameter or compute from a central date reference in your data.

Tips to implement in the learning hub’s practice system (needs a subscription)

To implement this in the practice system in the learning hub. You can use the system for the courses SACE11, SACS21, or SACM21. Before you start  create a BW live model and use the connection A4H, with the BW Query P00Q_BW_Q_VAR_HIER.

entry.title: END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 1 #ATR
entry.content:
#ATR (24) INFORMATIVE NOTE:

  • Created this blog to provide information on how to install ECC EHP8 System with Oracle 19c Database.
  • Please visit Uninstalling SAP System and Reuse SAP Server for new installation #ATR to have an overview of how to setup VM/OS from scratch for SAP.
  • Please visit QUICK CATCHUPS ON TOP-20-SWPM/SUM ERRORS – SAP INSTALLATION ON AIX – ORACLE #ATR to have an overview of SWPM/SUM Errors.

CHECK POINTS:

PART 1:

1) PREREQUISITE CHECK FOR INSTALLATION
2) STACK XML FILE GENERATION IN MAINTENANCE PLANNER
3) SWPM OPERATION [START] – INSTALLATION OF BASE PACK

Please refer link - END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 1 #ATR

PART 2:
4) ORACLE DATABASE INSTALLATION (IN MID OF SWPM)
5) ORACLE PATCH UPDATE WITH POST INSTALLATION SCRIPT (IN MID OF SWPM)
6) COMPLETION OF SWPM – INSTALLATION OF BASE PACK

Please refer link - END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 2 #ATR

PART 3:
7) SUM EXECUTION – EHP UPDATE WITH SAP NW

Please refer link - END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 3 #ATR


CHECK POINT 1: PREREQUISITE CHECK FOR INSTALLATION

  • In my scenario, we are having RHEL Linux Server 8.8 with SAP and Oracle Mount Points configured for SAP installation. FS /usr/sap will be increased based on the SUM/Trans Directory required while running the operation. You can allocate 55-65G in advance as well before starting Installation.

  • Make sure to check SAP – OS – Database compatibility before installing SAP systems. Please visit RHEL AND SAP UPGRADE WITH COMPATIBILITY CHECKS #ATR to have overview.
  • For our case, RHEL 8 (8.8) + Oracle 19c + EHP8 supported without any issues.
  • https://me.sap.com/notes/2478541 - 2478541 - Operating System Requirements for Oracle Database.
  • https://me.sap.com/notes/2171334/E - 2171334 - EHP8 for SAP ERP 6.0 SP Stacks - Release & Information Note

Check Point 1: PREREQUISITE CHECK FOR INSTALLATION Completed.

CHECK POINT 2: STACK XML FILE GENERATION IN MAINTENANCE PLANNER:

  • Select -> Plan a New System with EHP 8 [ SID – EC0 & EHP08 SP09 in my case]

  • Components will be shown w.r.t EHP08 – EHP, ERP, NW.
  • Dependencies and Stack SP version will be calculated automatically by MP.  
  • We can update SAP NW version if required without any EHP dependencies since NW version update will work without any issues. If we need to update EHP SP to higher SP (if available), Make sure to crosscheck whether you have crossed minimum SAP NW SP version required for that selected higher EHP SP level and its dependencies.
  • Click Next and Proceed further.

  • Select Your Database and other SAP files accordingly. (Mine – Oracle)
  • Mandatory – Oracle Kernel, Host Agent, IGS, SWPM, SUM.

  • We could see all EHP ERP Components, NW components and other SAP database/components files selected as per MP calculation.

  • Usually, SAP NW components will be PI_BASIS, ST-PI, SAP_GWFND, SAP_UI, SAP_ABA, SAP_BASIS, Service tools for SAP Basis and higher ST-A/PI, SAP_BW, Other Add Ons if exist with dependencies (Ex: CTS_PLUGIN).
  • We can also see SAP_APPL (ERP EHP) and SAP BASIS (SAP NW) were aligned with correct SP version by MP.

  • Click Next and Proceed Further.
  • Download Stack file and all software files (via Software Download Manager) and move files to your VM (In My case: RHEL Linux Server).

  • Approx Size – 22 GB.

  • Parallelly, Download Oracle Database Server and Client files as well for Oracle Installation.

  • All Software files downloaded and copied into our RHEL Server (includes SAPCAR extraction as well).

Check Point 2: STACK XML FILE GENERATION IN MAINTENANCE PLANNER Completed.

CHECK POINT 3: SWPM OPERATION – INSTALLATION OF BASE PACK

  • We can proceed SAP installation now. Extract SWPM.

  • For System Rename Alone, SWPM needs to be run with Short DNS since its need to detect correct application server and comply server hostname characters.
  • For more information on System Rename Scenarios, Please refer blog  SAP SYSTEM RENAME SCENARIOS #ATR
  • We can run SWPM with IP address for New Installation which we won’t be facing any issues.
  • Make sure to have Short DNS with <=13 characters as per SAP Compliance.

  • Login SWPM (URL) with root user.
  • Proceed with EHP8 Installation – Standard System (In my case)

3.1) DEFINE PARAMETER:

  • Selecting “Custom” mode to have an overview of all parameters.

  • Setting /sapmnt directory path.
  • We already have separate mount point (/sapmnt) with 10G. It’s sufficient for test-/demo system.

  • Provide FQDN (Ignore short DNS – Ignore.XXX.XXX.XXX)

  • Set Master Password

  • We can leave home directory which will set to default.  (/home/sidadm)

  • We are going to install Standalone Single Standard ECC System with Oracle Database in Same Host. Hence, leaving as it is and proceeded with default value.
  • Optional – Provide DBSID as same as SAP SID.

  • Specify your downloaded software package location.

  • Download DBATOOLS from SMP.

  • Make sure to place all files in single path/location which helps SWPM to detect all files in first analyzes.

  • Provide sapadm password.

INFO:

  • MP will provide Upgrade components to download directly. Base Installation files should be downloaded manually from our end.
  • EX – EHP8 FOR ERP 6.0 SP 0 (BASE INSTALLATION EXPORT) – Should be downloaded Manually.
  • EX – EHP8 FOR ERP 6.0 SP 1-8 (SP Update files) – Will be provided by MP to download directly.

  • Place all software (.rar) files in same/single directory and extract using exe application to have all export files (Count 11).
  • Move files to your VM (In my case – RHEL Linux Server).

SWPM CONTINUATION:

  • Provide LABELIDX.ASC path to detect all Installation Export files.

  • Choose Oracle version which we want to install.
  • Leaving other values as default. [Oracle 19c in my case]

  • Choose Oracle Database Software Owner – Oracle or Oraec0 (Recommendation - Prefer Oracle user for >=12c database)

  • Provide Database Administrator (OS User - oraSID) Account Password

  • Provide Database Administrator (OS User - oracle) Account Password

  • Provide ABAP SSFS – SAPSR3 Schema Password.
  • Leaving default value for DB Instance RAM (50%).
  • “Stop” Option selected for DB Patch update as well once DB created.

  • Using default listener port – 1521 with listener.ora and tnsnames.ora file.

  • Provide password for sys and system user.

  • Leaving default value – Enable declustering / depooling of all ABAP tables.

  • Minimum 3 parallel jobs should be provided for System Database Import (General load).

  • Select “Yes” for “Create DB Statistics” - Recommended

  • Provide Primary Application and ASCS Instance hostname and number.

  • Leaving default value as it is for Message server ports.

  • Provide DIA, BGND WPs based on your SAP System accordingly.

 

  • Provide ICM – webadm user password.

  • In my case, No SLD Configuration required. Make sure to provide your SLD Configuration accordingly based on your scenario.
  • EX – If you plan to add/connect your system to Solution Manager.

  • Choose Message Server Access Control List option accordingly.

  • Select “Web Dispatcher/Gateway – Embedded in ASCS” accordingly.
  • Usually, most of the projects use “Embedded Web Dispatcher” to allow limited URLs alone to connect SAP System.

  • Provide Web Dispatcher HTTP/HTTPs port accordingly.
  • Select Always if you plan to use SAP System for Productive purposes. In my case, Test/Demo – Selected “Depending on Incoming protocol with HTTP port as well”.

  • Error – /lib64/libstdc++.so.6: version `GLIBCXX_3.4.26' not found

  • Solution : https://access.redhat.com/solutions/6985203
  • Install compat-sap-c++-9 and do the symlink.

mkdir -p /usr/sap/lib
ln -s /opt/rh/SAP/lib64/compat-sap-c++-9.so /usr/sap/lib/libstdc++.so.6
chown -R SIDadm:sapsys /usr/sap/lib

  • Since, SIDadm was not created yet, leaving as root user with full access.
  • Make sure to change it to SIDadm once SAP System Installation completed.

  • Retry the SWPM failed step.
  • SWPM crossed the error. Choose “Key” accordingly.
  • In my case, I’m using default key since it’s test/demo system only.

  • Provide SAP System Users Passwords (DDIC and SAP* - Client 000/001)

  • Provide Transport Management (Recommended) – TMSADM user password.

  • Provide correct SPAM/SAINT Update version (In my case SAP BASIS 750 Latest)

  • Incorrect SPAM/SAINT file leads to various function module error which will be difficult to backtrack and conclude SPAM update RCA.
  • SWPM won’t show error/warning if you provide incorrect version since lower (default) version already available to SWPM.
  • In my case, Downloaded SAP BASIS 750 Latest (088) since our EHP8 SAP BASIS version will be 750.
  • Note: Download latest only if not downloaded via MP Stack.

  • We are installing newer SAP System from scratch. No need of ABAP transports. Choose accordingly based on your scenario.

 

  • Agree to SUM Extraction and Automatic Start of SUM.

  • Provide SUM file path.

  • Recommendation – To Install Additional Languages manually post installation of SAP System – Via SMLT and RSCPINST Report.
  • Proceeding with “No Additional SAP System Languages” in my case.

  • We have already downloaded Oracle Server Database and Client Files. Provide Client path accordingly.

  • Optional – Cleanup of OS system users from SAPINST group post SWPM completion.

3.2) REVIEW PARAMETER:

  • It’s just reviewing our provided parameter.

3.3) EXECUTE SERVICE:

  • SWPM will perform initial SAP Configurations and then waits for us to complete Oracle Database Installation (Manually) before proceeding Database SAP Instance Installation.

LIBRARY SYMLINK CHANGE:

  • Since SIDADM (ec0adm) user created, Changed FS permission of /usr/sap/lib from root to ec0adm.

IMPORTANT:

  • Default Instances will be created, and entries added in TNS and Listener ORA Files.
  • Hostname:1521 with instances EC0, EC0XDB, EC0PDB
  • KEY EXTPROC1521 (Not required)
  • Default Entries should be deleted. Else, SWPM will result in “TNS Port 1521 Already in Use” Error.
  • Just install Oracle Database manually from our end. Don’t mount/start Oracle Database Manually post installation.
  • Do Oracle Patch update only after SWPM shows/pauses on DB Patch update step.
  • Use Oracle user to start listener/db instance till SWPM/SUM Completion since SWPM uses Oracle user only.
  • You can change Oracle FS permission of Listener and TNS ORA files post SWPM/SUM completion from Oracle to OraSID to use SQLPLUS and start listener/db instance via ORAEC0 user.

Check Point 3: SWPM OPERATION [START] – INSTALLATION OF BASE PACK Completed

Please refer Part 2 for rest of the Installation steps.

Thanks for visiting !!!

entry.title: Meet up - São Paulo 2026-04-06
entry.content:
A todos da comunidade SAP de São Paulo: vamos ter a oportunidade de nos reunirmos após o SAP CodeJam, 6 de abril, que faz parte doSAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 - Brazil edition ! 

O encontro irá acontecer 6 de abril as 18:30 BRT, então junte-se a nós e faça parte do grupo! Você não precisa participar ou ter participadonos nossos eventos SAP CodeJam, esse encontro é para qualquer membro da SAP Community que seja curioso ou ativo - o nosso foco são os #developers.

Vamos aproveitar este tempo para falar das novidades do mundo SAP, dos projetos interessantes que já participarem e até mesmo o que tem de novidade sobre vocês mesmo!

Por favor, caso deseje participar, clique em "I will attend". Nós temos reservado uma mesa para 12 no "Bar do Juarez Brooklin".

Nós estamos felizes com a oportunidade de conectar as pessoas e ter um momento de lazer! Obs: Cada um é responsável por pagar sua comida e bebida

Clique aqui e saiba mais sobre o SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 - Brazil edition . 

E também convidamos de se juntar no grupo de São Paulo caso não seja membro ainda: São Paulo group

entry.title: END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 3 #ATR
entry.content:
#ATR (24) INFORMATIVE NOTE:

  • Created this blog to provide information on how to install ECC EHP8 System with Oracle 19c Database.
  • Please visit Uninstalling SAP System and Reuse SAP Server for new installation #ATR to have an overview of how to setup VM/OS from scratch for SAP.
  • Please visit QUICK CATCHUPS ON TOP-20-SWPM/SUM ERRORS – SAP INSTALLATION ON AIX – ORACLE #ATR to have an overview of SWPM/SUM Errors.

CHECK POINTS:

PART 1:

1) PREREQUISITE CHECK FOR INSTALLATION
2) STACK XML FILE GENERATION IN MAINTENANCE PLANNER
3) SWPM OPERATION [START] – INSTALLATION OF BASE PACK

Please refer link - END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 1 #ATR

PART 2:
4) ORACLE DATABASE INSTALLATION (IN MID OF SWPM)
5) ORACLE PATCH UPDATE WITH POST INSTALLATION SCRIPT (IN MID OF SWPM)
6) COMPLETION OF SWPM – INSTALLATION OF BASE PACK

Please refer link - END TO END INSTALLATION OF SAP EHP8 ERP SYSTEM WITH ORACLE DATABASE 19c ON RHEL 8.8 - PART 2 #ATR

PART 3:
7) SUM EXECUTION – EHP UPDATE WITH SAP NW

CHECK POINT 7: SUM EXECUTION – EHP UPDATE WITH SAP NW

  • Now, we are proceeding to perform SUM SP Update from EHP SP1 to SP9 with relevant updates of software components (includes ECC ERP, SAP NW as well).
  • We can start SUM separately (manual) as well to complete EHP Update without need of SWPM since SWPM process completed. Make sure to provide same Stack file for SUM if started manually later for EHP & NW update.
  • SWPM consider SUM Directory - /usr/sap/SID/SUM by default.
  • We can also use other directory or make separate LVM for SUM path alone to delete and reclaim space once SUM Execution completed.
  • In my case, I’m using /usr/sap FS itself since it’s test system and space will be increased accordingly.
  • Logon SUM with SIDADM (EC0ADM) user.
  • EX - https://servername:1129/lmsl/sumabap/EC0/doc/slui

  • Make sure to have atleast 55-65G free space in /usr/sap (includes trans/sumabap) directory to proceed update process.
  • Also, provide full 777 access to downloaded Software files.

  • You can download and upload notes manually if you are planning to do post installation steps (SAP Note Download Connectivity, SAP Support Portal) once completion of SUM process.

  • All mentioned notes implemented in system. Repeat SUM failed process.

  • Configuration Checks completed successfully.

  • Checks completed successfully.

  • Lock Development Environment.

  • We can release our note implementation TR (changes) even if captured in Local Transport requests since STMS configuration – Transport Routes yet to be setup with corresponding SAP Systems/Virtual System to release TRs and get Data files &Co files.
  • We can setup STMS Transport routes and includes this note changes (objects) later in the new TR/Transport of Copies which can be created with Target System (Corresponding SAP System/Virtual System- Relevant Transport Route) to get data files and co files.

  • Released TR – Local Change Transport requests – Without Data/Co Files.

  • Repeat SUM failed process.

  • Backup system based on your scenarios accordingly.

  • SUM will replace kernel files even if existing kernel have same version.

  • Importing entries into control tables take several hours to complete.
  • Don’t cancel/kill SUM/OS level processes. Even if SUM stopped due to network connection issue, Wait for the completion of the OS level process.

  • We could see R3trans import process completed successfully.
  • As of now, /usr/sap/trans occupied 19G for update and /usr/sap/SID occupied 33G for ABAP and SUM Files.

  • We can start/resume SUM process with “Check Status Again”.

  • SUM Execution step completed successfully.
  • Final à SUM Dir occupied 38G and trans Dir occupied 19 G – Total – 57G

  • We won’t face issues mostly in Post Processing steps.

  • We are installing new EHP8 ECC System with no transport provided during SWPM operations.

  • SUM Procedure completed successfully.

  • Errors requiring revision – “Create statement view "ACMAUTE639F7F4D8" could not be generated” can be ignored as per Note 2700577 - https://me.sap.com/notes/0002700577

  • We have successfully completed SWPM and SUM Operations.
  • We can cleanup SUM Directory since operations completed. Select “Cleanup” from Menu (Available near Breakpoints)

  • Post cleanup, SUM Dir file size reduced from 38G to <1GB. /usr/sap/trans occupied 20G

  • Since all operations completed, we can change TNS, Listener Ora Files Owner from Oracle OINSTALL to ORAEC0 DBA as well as restart database via Oraec0 user.
  • PATH - /oracle/EC0/19/network/log/listener.log
  • PATH - /oracle/EC0/19/network/admin/* (especially TNS, Listener, SQLNET)
  • PATH – Listener Socket Files Access - /var/tmp/.oracle/*
  • Start Listener/DB Instance via ORADBA user

  • ORAEC0 DBA user has default SAP/ORACLE DB ENV Variables to connect DB/ Restart Listener without setting Env Variables manually as like Oracle user.

Check Point 7: SUM EXECUTION – EHP UPDATE WITH SAP NW Completed.

SYS INFO:

 

WE HAVE SUCCESSFULLY INSTALLED ERP EHP8 SP09 WITH ORACLE 19c (19.24) ON RHEL 8.8

Thanks for visiting !!!

entry.title: Top 5 Mistakes I’ve Seen in SAP SuccessFactors Story Reports (and How We Fixed Them)
entry.content:
Introduction

If you’ve worked on SAP SuccessFactors Story Reports for some time, you already know how powerful they are. You can build very clean dashboards and give meaningful insights to HR and business teams.

But honestly, in my experience, Story Reports are also one of those tools where small mistakes can create big confusion.

In one of my recent implementations, we faced multiple issues where the report was technically correct, but the output didn’t make sense to business users. Most of the time, the issue was not data—it was how we designed the report.

Here are some common mistakes I’ve personally seen , and how you can avoid them.

1. Overusing or Misusing Filters

What I’ve seen:
In one report, we had filters applied at both page level and widget level. Everything looked fine during development, but when the HR team used it, they started saying some employees were missing.

After debugging, we realized some filters were conflicting with each other.

What happens:
• Data looks incomplete
• Users assume there is a data issue

What worked for us:
• We reduced unnecessary widget-level filters
• Kept most filters at page level
• Always previewed data after applying filters

Also, one small thing that helped ,we started adding a short text note explaining what filters are applied. It reduced a lot of confusion.


2. Using the Wrong Join Type

Where we went wrong:
While building a headcount report, we initially used an Inner Join between Employment Info and Job Info.

The report looked fine… until we compared numbers with another report. Headcount was lower than expected.

Root cause:
Some employees were getting dropped because of the join condition.

Fix:
• Switched to Left Outer Join
• Used Employment Info as the base table
• Validated counts with a Table Report

Since then, one rule I follow:
-> If it’s a headcount-related report, I’m extra careful with joins.


3. Ignoring Role-Based Permissions (RBP)

Real issue we faced:
We shared a report with HRBPs, but they kept saying “No Data”.

Initially, we thought it was a bug.

But later, we found:
• They didn’t have access to some fields used in the report

Lesson learned:
Sharing the report is not enough.

What we started doing:
• Coordinating early with RBP team
• Checking schema and field permissions
• Using Proxy to test from end-user perspective

This step alone saved us from multiple escalations later.

4. Misinterpreting KPIs and Measures

Classic mistake (we also did this):
Using “Sum” for salary without checking duplicate records.

Result? Salary numbers were inflated.

Another case — active employee count was wrong because terminated employees were not properly filtered.

What helped:
• Cross-checking with existing reports
• Reviewing aggregation logic carefully
• Creating calculated columns where needed

Now before finalizing any KPI, I always ask:
-> “Does this number make sense from a business point of view?”

5. Ignoring User Experience

Biggest realization:
Even a technically perfect report can fail if users don’t understand it.

In one project, we created a detailed dashboard—but users stopped using it because it felt too complex.

What we improved later:
• Added text widgets to explain sections
• Simplified layout
• Used consistent formatting

We started treating reports like a story, not just data.

And honestly, adoption improved a lot after that.

Conclusion

Story Reports are extremely powerful, but they require careful design and testing.
From my experience, most issues don’t come from the system—they come from how we configure filters, joins, permissions, and KPIs.

Now before publishing any report, I usually do a quick checklist:
• Filters validated
• Joins verified
• RBP tested
• Numbers cross-checked

These small checks have saved me a lot of time (and a lot of follow-up calls).

If you’re working on Story Reports, I’d definitely recommend spending extra time on validation, it makes a huge difference in how the business trusts your report.

entry.title: Re: Managing Authorization for Plan Customer Projects App
entry.content:
Dear team, hope you're well!

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, restricting the Plan Customer Projects (F0719) app to specific editable fields (Customer Reference Number and Billing Due Date) while making the rest read-only is achieved through Business Role Restrictions (IAM). However, the standard "Plan Customer Projects" app is designed for project editing, and field-level restriction (e.g., locking only the description while allowing only the Reference Number) is quite limited. 
To achieve your requirement, use the approach, described in SAP KBA: 3535908 - Access Control Restriction for "Billing" tab of "Plan Customer Projects" App in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me
You may also consider reviewing the documentation for BAdIs such as App Extensibility: Plan Customer Projects | SAP Help Portal (Search under the BAdI Description: Check Authorization to Edit Project) and consider if it's fitted to your scenario. 
Hope this information helped bring some light into your query. 
Kind regards,
Victoria

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - Blueprint (Part 2 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

In Part 1 , we explored the transformative potential of the Electronic Bill of Lading in international trade, highlighting its impact on sustainability, enhanced supply chain transparency, and accelerated business processes.

Part 2 presents the building blocks used to implement the solution and includes an architecture diagram illustrating how the SAP BTP services and components are integrated.

 

1. System Context

The ocean carrier already operates an established core system that manages transactional data such as bookings, shipping instructions, and transport events on SAP Cloud ERP( S/4HANA Transportation Management).

SAP Cloud ERP

 

2. SAP Business Technology Platform

The foundation of this solution is SAP BTP, a cloud platform that brings together application development, data management, integration, and AI capabilities. It provides the core infrastructure and services needed to build enterprise applications.

To support the transition to digital trade, the Electronic Bill of Lading capability is implemented on SAP BTP as a side-by-side extension.

 

3. Electronic Bill of Lading: TradeTrust Framework

The open-source TradeTrust Framework  serve as the foundation for implementing a fully digital and verifiable Electronic Bill of Lading solution. It can be deployed as a Node.js application on Cloud Foundry, which provides the runtime for developing and running cloud-native applications on SAP BTP.

On this PoC, the TradeTrust framework uses the Ethereum blockchain whereby the ERC-721 provides a widely-used smart contract API used on non-fungible tokens (NFTs) to allow transfer of ownership whilst providing assurance of integrity, singularity, and control.

Blockchain technology is a decentralized digital ledger system that records and verifies transactions across a network of computers in a secure and transparent manner. It consists of a chain of blocks, each containing a list of transactions thatare linked and secured using cryptographic techniques. Once a block is added to the chain, its data become immutable through a distributed ledger system, ensuring the integrity and permanence of the transaction records. This immutability and transparency enable blockchain to facilitate the exchange of anything of value, whether tangible assets including physical goods or intangible items such as digital assets.[11]

 

4. Terminal Operating System

Terminal Operating System (TOS) is the port terminal’s operational platform that manage and monitor container and vessel activities. Leveraging IoT-enabled equipment and sensors, the TOS generates real-time operational events such as container loading, vessel arrival, and departure. These events are distributed through a publish–subscribe model, pushing notifications to a configured endpoint. To ensure authenticity and integrity, the messages are encrypted and digitally signed.

5. Event-Driven Architecture

SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP provides the capabilities that enable Event-Driven Architecture within the solution. The Cloud Integration is the middleware layer responsible for routing, transforming, and exchanging messages between external systems and SAP services. Under the hood, it uses the open-source Apache Camel Framework, enabling integration flows based on enterprise integration patterns.

For event orchestration SAP Event Mesh is the message broker, it facilitates asynchronous communication by receiving events from the terminal/port and routing them to the subscribed consumer Events-To-Business Actions Framework, that leverages the decisions capability of SAP Build Process Automation to initiate business actions.

 

6. Automation

For the process automation, a workflow is managed on SAP Build Process Automation 

 

7. UI

Stakeholders access the application through SAP Build Work Zone, where authentication is handled by SAP Cloud Identity Services integrated with a custom Identity Provider (IdP) that validates blockchain wallet signatures. Authorization is then enforced via role collections, ensuring access only to permitted functionalities.

 

8. Diagram

  1. The Terminal Operating System generates an operational event(Load, Departure, Arrival). The event is published to the event broker SAP Event Mesh.
  2. Event-to-Business-Actions framework is subscribed to SAP Event Mesh topics to receive the events.
  3. Based on the rule outcome, the processor triggers a workflow on SAP Build Process Automation to orchestrate the business process.
  4. The workflow calls SAP Integration Suite to start the process.
  5. An Integration Flow retrieves the required Ocean Freight Booking from SAP S/4HANA and builds the payload to issue the eBL.
  6. The Integration Flow invokes the TradeTrust Framework.
  7. The Electronic Bill of Lading is issued on Ethereum Network.

 

9. Data Context

 

Conclusion:

Part 2 outlined the essential building blocks and architectural components for implementing the eBL solution on SAP Business Technology Platform.

In Part 3, I’ll walk through a complete end-to-end global trade scenario, illustrating how the architecture operates in practice, from container loading event to eBL issuance, ownership transfer, and final surrender.

entry.title: Re: SAP datasphere task chain: sql procedure not visible
entry.content:
There are some points can be check.

1. Re-check the privilege assignment

2. Create a minimal test procedure to narrow down the scope

To determine whether this is a privilege issue or something specific to the procedure definition, can have a quick test:

  • Create a very simple test procedure in the same Open SQL schema (for example, without referencing other tables or objects).
  • Check whether this test procedure becomes visible during Task Chain modeling.

If the test procedure is visible, this usually indicates that the space-to-schema access and EXECUTE privilege are generally working, and the issue may be related to the original procedure’s definition or its dependent objects.

If the test procedure is not visible, this strongly points to a remaining authorization or execution-identity issue.

entry.title: Re: Valuation of produced material - moving average price
entry.content:
Good day @Anja_1 

Thank you for your question.

The answer to your question on the availability of a customisation to make the order output use total planned cost instead of current MAP is NO, not in the Public Cloud design for a V (average moving) price material. The output recipient is preliminarily valuated, and the system handles order differences later through settlements or event-based production accounting.

The Public Cloud provides two options:

  • Option 1 is for you to change the material to standard price control. This best supports in house manufactured materials. That also ensures your preliminary goods receipt closely follows the standard costing process.
  • Option 2 is to keep MAP but use Actual Costing. If your business needs valuation which is close to your actual production cost, then the recommended approach is to use 33Q Actual Costing. Here, the system handles actual price valuation in the actual costing run, not by changing the preliminary goods receipt valuation logic of the production order itself.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4032610758dc437089f0c28320eec93f/01500bf05468400b873c792b38c44161.html?locale=en-US

3599914 - About the transition from the scope item BEI to the scope item 3F0 - SAP for Me

3445478 - Unable to find actual costing related APPs - - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

A constraint you are facing in your specific design is that the same material number is both consumed in the order and received again as output. This makes the price behaviour even more sensitive for a MAP material, because the system keeps using the moving average logic for the same stock identity. So, that result you see is consistent with standard MAP logic rather than the logic that drives a routing based finished good valuation.

So, if your business process is really an adjustment or refurbishment of the same material, then consider whether a rework order process is a better fit than producing the same MAP material into itself.

See the following references for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/bbd9b6ce0be14516ad7868da0982024a.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4032610758dc437089f0c28320eec93f/9d75eaf09b2d4d3fae127b4e9f1fc69a.html?locale=en-US

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Top 5 Mistakes I’ve Seen in SAP SuccessFactors Story Reports (and How We Fixed Them)
entry.content: Thanks for sharing this—really helpful! 🙌

entry.title: Re: Can MRP prioritize one BOM alternative component and...
entry.content:
Hello @FaisalMaayeh 

Thank you for your question.

Question 1: In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, can MRP itself recommend the alternative component automatically based on stock availability?

Answer: Not in that way you described it. The reason is, MRP does not behave like a stock aware decision engine that first consumes B and only if B is short automatically replans with C as a fallback. MRP uses the usage probability to determine dependent requirements. See KBA 3316105 for more insights.

So, if your requirement is specifically for MRP to check actual stock of B first, and only if B is unavailable, automatically propose C instead, then the answer is no, not as standard MRP fallback option in Public Cloud.

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/86fab5c5a10440f0a9dccdc6d761b196.html?locale=en-US

Question 2: Or is this only handled later by the planner in the production order?

Answer: The automatic stock-based fallback is not a standard behaviour. In practice, this requirement is typically resolved after planning through planner review and operation decision making rather than by expecting MRP itself to automatically make the substitution.

Question 3: If standard MRP does not support this exact fallback behavior, what is the best-practice approach in Public Cloud?

Answer: The best practice in Public Cloud is usually one of these:

  • Option A is for you to maintain alternative items in the BOM and let the planner decided the order
  • Option B is to use product substitution where the business scenario is product substitution, not BOM component substitution.
  • Option C is for you to review whether you need different planning design. If your business requirement is very strict and must be automatic, stock sensitive, and rules based during planning, then standard alternative item BOM may not be sufficient. In that case, you will need to redesign around the planning model rather than expecting MRP to provide a custom fallback algorithm

See the following reference for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/8a7dea8cc1a4421e97acb2463f544b2b.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Introducing the First Demand Handover Process in SAP IBP Harmonized Planning Area
entry.content:
We are pleased to share that the first scenario for handing over demand between time‑series planning and order-based planning is now available in the SAP IBP Harmonized Planning Area (HPA). This process helps organizations create a more seamless flow of information from strategic and tactical planning into operational execution.

The new process uses forecast consumption based on sales order data coming through Real-Time Integration (RTI). After consumption, the remaining open forecast is transferred from weekly planning to the daily level so it can be used directly by order-based planning.

To enable real-time forecast consumption, the process relies on key figure calculations, ensuring that customers have a consistent and transparent way to prepare demand before it hands over into order-based planning.

With this first scenario in place, customers can run demand, S&OP, and operational planning on harmonized data while maintaining clear ownership between the planning layers. It reduces manual steps and helps planners ensure that the demand used in supply and order planning remains aligned with the latest business inputs.

Concept for Demand Handover on PRODLOC level

This section outlines key design considerations and best practices for configuring and utilizing forecast consumption within SAP Integrated Business Planning (IBP) at the Product-Location (PRODLOC) planning level.
The following points detail various aspects of this process, including system behavior, recommended configurations, and specific scenarios.

  • Time-series planning is performed on the level of WKPRODLOCTSPCUST (week, product, location, time-series planning customer)
  • Forecast consumption is configured on the level of WKPRODLOC using the sales order information integrated via Real-Time Integration (RTI)
    • If forecast consumption is configured using the simplified key figure calculation, we recommend keeping the calculation graph small. This approach helps mitigating potential performance impacts, as complex graphs can lead to time-consuming operations. Furthermore, note that restrictions apply to the output key figures for the IBP_CONSUMPTION function (see SAP help)
  • For periods shorter than six weeks the forecast from demand sensing can be used to leverage its short-term accuracy. For periods beyond six weeks the forecast from IBP demand can be used.

We will describe here a case where it is not possible to assign a sales order with an Order-Based Planning (OBP) customer to a TSP customer. Then the aggregation is only possible on WKPRODLOC/DAYPRODLOC level.

  • If demand sensing is not in use, the recommendation is to disaggregate the open forecast on daily level using the number of working days per calendar week.
  • The open forecast, resulting from the forecast consumption, is aggregated on the level of DAYPRODLOC (i.e. without any TSP or OBP customer information)
  • As the automated forecast consumption from SAP7F is not available in HPA we recommend using the keyfigure calculation or forecast consumption job.
  • The remaining open forecast can either be directly used as input to OBP via a calculated key figure or through a release process using a copy operator. Note that restrictions apply to the output key figures of the IBP_CONSUMPTION function.

entry.title: SAP Business AI in Retail - Partner live expert session
entry.content:
Retail + AI = massive opportunity.

But here’s the catch: opportunity only matters if you can act on it.

In this live session, we’re getting straight to the point; what’s happening in retail, where AI fits, and how you can bring it into your deals today.

Expect a fast-paced walkthrough of key market trends, sharp use cases, and a live demo on SDE 2.0.  Plus (and this is key), we’ll break down how to actually sell AI in retail, who to engage, what to say, and where to get support.

Whether you’re already in retail conversations or just getting started, this session will give you something concrete to take away.

Spots are limited, so don’t wait too long.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013493.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AIInnovation #RetailTech #SAPAI

entry.title: Re: CBC Scope Item Deletion: Does it remain active in the...
entry.content:
Good day @UmenoYagi 

Thank you for your question.

Question: If a scope item that was previously activated in CBC is deleted by SAP, does it remain active and functional in the customer system?

Answer: Yes for depreciated scope item, when you have already activated it in your system. It does not automatically disappear from your tenant. The only thing you should have in mind is, due to the depreciation, the functionality will remain static. In other words, it will not be updated by SAP, and some functions may still remain usable.

Regarding which changes occur, the main change is usually:

  • The scope item is no longer available for new activation or new scoping
  • Your current related configuration is kept
  • As mentioned above SAP will no longer update that scope item.
  • Ensure you monitor the release information and the availability for successor or impacts you may experience due to the removal.

Here is a clarification you need to be aware of.

There is a difference between:

  1. Deprecated/no longer offered for scoping. In this situation, active customer configuration is not deleted and not updated.
  2. A feature or app being fully removed in a future release. In this scenario, make sure you consult the SAP What’s New documentation.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Is it mandatory to tag pay component in Pension fund even if storing for info purpose only
entry.content:
HI All , 

Our client is using Global Benefits to store and reflect info at single platform. For Pension - admin are uploading the file with value that employee has opted in start of month and captured in separate tool. 

Client does not want any pay component to created or any data to be propagated for Pension to comp info hence , we have keep it as Benefit integration and over to this , as client also doesnt want to create irrelevant pay components as part of foundation data . 

Hence , we have created the pension fund without pay component which system currently allows as well. 

But we wanted to ensure - if this is against good practice or if there are any other impacts with such config. 

As of now , we do not see any challenges as this suffices the client requirement. 

 

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Global Benefits

entry.title: Re: SAP datasphere task chain: sql procedure not visible
entry.content:
Hello Severilar,

If you want to add some procedure in Task Chain,  Please ensure that there are no parameters in the procedure or that the following parameter types are used：

  • "NCHAR", "STRING", "NVARCHAR", "ALPHANUM", "SHORTTEXT", "INT", "TINYINT", "BIGINT", "CHAR", "DECIMAL", "SMALLDECIMAL", "INTEGER", "SMALLINT", "REAL", "DOUBLE", "FLOAT", "VARCHAR", "BOOLEAN", "DATE", "TIME", "SECONDDATE", "TIMESTAMP"

entry.title: Re: SAP for me error message when displaying installation...
entry.content:
Hi customer,

In order to check the installation data in SAP for Me, the S-user needs 'Manage Installations' authorization assigned either on CCC level, customer number level or installation number level.

Please refer to KBA 3469559 - Authorization required to view my company's installation numbers - SAP for Me. 

Please kindly check and make sure your S-user has this authorization properly assigned.

If not, then please request for the authorization using the self-service feature as introduced in KBA 2891528 - How to use the Self-Service Authorization Request - SAP for Me.

Hope this helps.

Best regards,
SAP Support
